text: The surgeon performs a complete mesocolic excision using a medial to lateral approach for cecum and proximal ascending colon tumors. The ileocolic vein and artery are dissected, clipped, and divided at their connection to the superior mesenteric vein. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenal surface, and the ileum is transected ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve. Middle colic vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided. Indocyanine green is used to assess colon perfusion, and the transverse colon is transected with robotic staplers, completing the resection.

The surgeon performs a complete mesocolic excision using a medial to lateral approach for cecum and proximal ascending colon tumors. The ileocolic vein and artery are dissected, clipped, and divided at their connection to the superior mesenteric vein. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenal surface, and the ileum is transected 10 centimeters from the ileocecal valve. Middle colic vessels are identified, dissected, clipped, and divided. Indocyanine green is used to assess colon perfusion, and the transverse colon is transected with robotic staplers, completing the resection.

The surgeon employs a top-to-down no-touch technique for tumors in the distal ascending hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon. The dissection begins with the division of the omentum from the stomach, revealing the pancreas. The dissection continues inferior to the pancreas, exposing the superior mesenteric vein. The middle colic vein is dissected, clipped, and divided, followed by the gastroepiploic artery. The gastrocolic trunk is identified, dissected, clipped, and divided as the dissection progresses along the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon employs a top-to-down no-touch technique to dissect distal ascending hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon tumors. The omentum is divided from the stomach, revealing the pancreas and continuing dissection inferiorly. The superior mesenteric vein is exposed, and the middle colic vein is dissected, clipped, and divided. The gastroepiploic artery is similarly dissected, clipped, and divided. The gastrocolic trunk is identified, dissected, clipped, and divided. The middle colic artery is reached, dissected, clipped, and divided. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenum, and the hepatic flexure is taken down. The superior mesenteric vein is dissected from the pancreas down to the ileocolic vein, with the ileocolic vessels identified, clipped, and divided from the medial side.

The surgeon reaches the middle colic artery, which is dissected, clipped, and divided. Using the top-to-down no-touch technique, lymph nodes in the infrapyloric region and around the pancreas are safely dissected within an intact mesocolon. The mesocolon is separated from the duodenum, and the hepatic flexure is mobilized. The superior mesenteric vein is dissected from the pancreas down to the ileocolic vein. After completing the superior dissection, the ileocolic vessels are identified from the medial side, clipped, and divided.

Indocyanine green is used to assess the perfusion of the colon, followed by transection with a robotic stapler. The ileum is then transected, and the colon is freed from its lateral attachments, completing the resection. For intracorporeal anastomosis, the ends of the ileum and colon are aligned, and two openings are created for the robotic stapler. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed, and the remaining openings are closed with a V-like suture.

Indocyanine green is used to check the perfusion of the colon, followed by transection with a robotic stapler. The ileum is also transected, and the colon is freed from its lateral attachments, completing the resection. For intracorporeal anastomosis, the ends of the ileum and colon are aligned, and two openings are created for the robotic stapler. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed, and the remaining openings are closed with a V-like suture.
